Kiro CLI implements the [Agent Client Protocol (ACP)][53], an open standard that enables AI agents to work with any compatible editor. This means you can use Kiro's agentic capabilities in JetBrains IDEs, Zed, and other ACP-compatible editors.

## What is ACP?[][54]

AI coding agents and editors are tightly coupled, but interoperability isn't the default. Each editor must build custom integrations for every agent, and agents must implement editor-specific APIs. This creates integration overhead, limited compatibility, and developer lock-in.

ACP solves this by providing a standardized protocol for agent-editor communication—similar to how the Language Server Protocol (LSP) standardized language server integration. Agents that implement ACP work with any compatible editor, and editors that support ACP gain access to all ACP-compatible agents.

## Quick start[][55]

Run Kiro as an ACP agent:

bash

`kiro-cli acp
`

To use a specific agent configuration:

bash

`kiro-cli acp --agent my-agent
`

The agent communicates over stdin/stdout using JSON-RPC 2.0. Configure your editor to spawn this command, and you're ready to go.

## Editor setup[][56]

Kiro CLI can be used as an ACP agent in any editor that supports the protocol.

**Info**

Use the full path to `kiro-cli` in your editor configuration. IDEs often don't inherit your shell's PATH, so commands like `kiro-cli` may not be found. Run `which kiro-cli` to find the path on your system (commonly `~/.local/bin/kiro-cli` on Linux/macOS).

### JetBrains IDEs[][57]

JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ IDEA, WebStorm, PyCharm, etc.) support ACP through AI Assistant. See the [JetBrains ACP documentation][58] for full details.

To add Kiro as a custom agent:
1. Open the AI Chat tool window
2. Click the settings button and select **Add Custom Agent**
3. Add the following to `~/.jetbrains/acp.json`:

json

`{
  "agent_servers": {
    "Kiro Agent": {
      "command": "/full/path/to/kiro-cli",
      "args": ["acp"]
    }
  }
}
`

The agent will appear in the AI Chat mode selector.

### Zed[][59]

Zed supports ACP agents natively. See the [Zed external agents documentation][60] for full details. Add the following to your Zed settings (`~/.config/zed/settings.json`):

json

`{
  "agent_servers": {
    "Kiro Agent": {
      "type": "custom",
      "command": "~/.local/bin/kiro-cli",
      "args": ["acp"],
      "env": {}
    }
  }
}
`

Select "Kiro Agent" from the agent picker in Zed's AI panel.

### Other editors[][61]

Any editor supporting ACP can integrate Kiro by spawning `kiro-cli acp` and communicating via JSON-RPC over stdio. See the [ACP specification][62] for protocol details.

## Supported ACP methods[][63]

Kiro CLI implements the following ACP methods, giving you access to session management, model selection, and streaming responses when using Kiro through any ACP-compatible editor.

### Core protocol[][64]

───────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────
Method             │Description                                        
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`initialize`       │Initialize the connection and exchange capabilities
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`session/new`      │Create a new chat session                          
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`session/load`     │Load an existing session by ID                     
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`session/prompt`   │Send a prompt to the agent                         
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`session/cancel`   │Cancel the current operation                       
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`session/set_mode` │Switch agent mode (e.g., different agent configs)  
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`session/set_model`│Change the model for the session                   
───────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────

### Agent capabilities[][65]

The Kiro ACP agent advertises these capabilities during initialization:
* `loadSession: true` - Supports loading existing sessions
* `promptCapabilities.image: true` - Supports image content in prompts

### Session updates[][66]

The agent sends these session update types via `session/notification`:

───────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────
Update Type        │Description                                  
───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────
`AgentMessageChunk`│Streaming text/content from the agent        
───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────
`ToolCall`         │Tool invocation with name, parameters, status
───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────
`ToolCallUpdate`   │Progress updates for running tools           
───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────
`TurnEnd`          │Signals the agent turn has completed         
───────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────

## Kiro extensions[][67]

Kiro extends ACP with custom methods (prefixed with `_kiro.dev/` per the ACP spec) to expose Kiro-specific features like [slash commands][68], [MCP servers][69], and [context compaction][70]. Clients that don't support these extensions can safely ignore them—they're optional enhancements.

**Warning**

These extensions are experimental and subject to change in future releases.

### Slash commands[][71]

──────────────────────────────┬────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Method                        │Type        │Description                                                    
──────────────────────────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/commands/execute`  │Request     │Execute a slash command (e.g., `/agent swap`, `/context add`)  
──────────────────────────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/commands/options`  │Request     │Get autocomplete suggestions for a partial command             
──────────────────────────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/commands/available`│Notification│Sent after session creation with the list of available commands
──────────────────────────────┴────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

### MCP server events[][72]

──────────────────────────────────┬────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Method                            │Type        │Description                                                                  
──────────────────────────────────┼────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/mcp/oauth_request`     │Notification│Provides OAuth URL when an MCP server requires authentication                
──────────────────────────────────┼────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/mcp/server_initialized`│Notification│Indicates an MCP server has finished initializing and its tools are available
──────────────────────────────────┴────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

### Session management[][73]

─────────────────────────────┬────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Method                       │Type        │Description                                          
─────────────────────────────┼────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/compaction/status`│Notification│Reports progress when compacting conversation context
─────────────────────────────┼────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/clear/status`     │Notification│Reports status when clearing session history         
─────────────────────────────┼────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_session/terminate`         │Notification│Terminates a subagent session                        
─────────────────────────────┴────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

## Example: Initialize connection[][74]

Here's how an ACP client initializes a connection with Kiro:

json

`// Client sends initialize request
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 0,
  "method": "initialize",
  "params": {
    "protocolVersion": 1,
    "clientCapabilities": {
      "fs": {
        "readTextFile": true,
        "writeTextFile": true
      },
      "terminal": true
    },
    "clientInfo": {
      "name": "my-editor",
      "version": "1.0.0"
    }
  }
}

// Kiro responds with capabilities
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 0,
  "result": {
    "protocolVersion": 1,
    "agentCapabilities": {
      "loadSession": true,
      "promptCapabilities": {
        "image": true
      }
    },
    "agentInfo": {
      "name": "kiro-cli",
      "version": "1.5.0"
    }
  }
}
`

After initialization, create a session and start prompting:

json

`// Create a new session
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 1,
  "method": "session/new",
  "params": {
    "cwd": "/home/user/my-project",
    "mcpServers": []
  }
}

// Send a prompt
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 2,
  "method": "session/prompt",
  "params": {
    "sessionId": "sess_abc123",
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "text",
        "text": "Explain this codebase"
      }
    ]
  }
}
`

## Session storage[][75]

ACP sessions are persisted to disk at:

`~/.kiro/sessions/cli/
`

Each session creates two files:
* `<session-id>.json` - Session metadata and state
* `<session-id>.jsonl` - Event log (conversation history)

## Logging[][76]

ACP agent logs are written to the standard Kiro log location:

────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────
Platform│Location                                 
────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────
macOS   │`$TMPDIR/kiro-log/kiro-chat.log`         
────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────
Linux   │`$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/kiro-log/kiro-chat.log`
────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────

Control log verbosity with environment variables:

bash

`KIRO_LOG_LEVEL=debug kiro-cli acp
KIRO_CHAT_LOG_FILE=/path/to/custom.log kiro-cli acp
`

## Related[][77]
* [Interactive Chat][78] - Uses ACP internally
* [MCP Integration][79] - MCP servers can be passed to ACP sessions
* [CLI Commands Reference][80]

results.title: The LSP for AI Coding Agents
results.snippet: At its core, the Agent Client Protocol is a unified JSON-based interface between code editors (the client) and AI coding agents (the server). Think of it as a translator that lets any ACP-compatible editor work with any ACP-compliant AI agent, regardless of who built either component.

results.title: Skills Marketplace · LobeHub
results.snippet: The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) is a standardized JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol enabling communication between AI coding agents and editor clients. It's similar to LSP but for AI agents.

results.title: Plug-and-Play AI Agents for Code Editors · CodeStandUp 👨‍💻
results.snippet: Agents that support ACP can work with any compatible editor. Editors that support ACP can connect to the entire ecosystem of ACP-compatible agents. This independence allows both sides to innovate while giving developers the freedom to select the best tools for their workflow.

results.title: Github
results.snippet: The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) is a standardized communication framework between code editors (IDEs) and AI coding agents, similar to how the Language Server Protocol (LSP) unified language server integration. ACP enables any compatible agent to work with any compatible editor without custom integrations.

results.title: Zed debuts Agent Client Protocol to connect AI coding agents to any editor
results.snippet: With the launch of ACP, that system becomes interoperable: any IDE or editor that implements ACP can connect to agents like Claude Code or Gemini CLI, without needing a bespoke integration for each.

results.title: Zed — Agent Client Protocol
results.snippet: The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) is an open standard that enables any agent to integrate seamlessly with any editing environment.

results.title: Agent Client Protocol: Making Agentic Editing Portable
results.snippet: The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) is an open effort by Zed Industries to standardize how code editors talk to AI coding agents. If the Language Server Protocol decoupled languages from editors, ACP aims to decouple agents from editors, so any ACP-capable agent can work in any ACP-capable editor.

results.title: Agent Client Protocol: A protocol for connecting any editor to any agent
results.snippet: Agents that implement ACP work with any compatible editor. Editors that support ACP gain access to the entire ecosystem of ACP-compatible agents. This decoupling allows both sides to innovate independently while giving developers the freedom to choose the best tools for their workflow.

results.title: Agent Client Protocol
results.snippet: The ACP Registry is an easy way for developers to distribute their ACP-compatible agents to any client that speaks the protocol. This is a curated set of agents, including only the ones that support authentication. Visit the registry repository on GitHub to learn more about it.

results.title: JetBrains × Zed: Open Interoperability for AI Coding Agents in Your IDE
results.snippet: The ACP protocol makes agents editor-agnostic, so agents work anywhere you code. Full control. Going forward, your IDE will mediate access to files, the terminal, and other tools via the ACP protocol – you decide what runs.

# Specialized IDEs deserve AI too: Kiro adopts ACP

By
BR

Brian Beach

Tech Lead

I'm excited to share that Kiro CLI now supports the Agent Client Protocol (ACP), which means you can use Kiro's agentic AI capabilities directly into other IDEs. IDEs that currently support ACP include Eclipse, Emacs, JetBrains IDEs, Neovim, Toad, Zed, and more. For me, this solves a real workflow problem that is important to me.

Let me be clear: Kiro is my daily driver for application development. The spec-driven workflows, hooks, and steering files have fundamentally changed how I build features. But here's the thing—when I'm deep in database work, I prefer a purpose build editor. The schema visualization, query execution plans, database-specific autocomplete, and connection management are purpose-built for SQL work. I don't want to configure database extensions in Kiro every time I switched projects or databases. Now, with ACP support, I can use Kiro in my preferred database management tool, JetBrians DataGrip.

## What is ACP?[][12]

The [Agent Client Protocol (ACP)][13] standardizes communication between code editors and AI coding agents. Think of it like LSP (Language Server Protocol), but for AI agents instead of language servers. Before ACP, every agent-editor combination required custom integration work. Agents only worked with specific editors. ACP changes this by providing a common protocol that any agent and any editor can implement. The practical benefit: agents that implement ACP work with any compatible editor. You pick your agent, you pick your IDE or ACP compatible application, and they just work together.

## Setting up Kiro in DataGrip[][14]

Getting Kiro running in DataGrip takes about two minutes.

First, make sure you have the [Kiro CLI][15] installed. If you haven't already, check out the [installation instructions][16] and make sure that you are [authenticated][17].

Next, open DataGrip and navigate to the AI Chat tool window. Click the menu button (three dots) in the upper-right corner and select "Add Custom Agent."

Here's a recap of our conversation:

## 1. Internal Amazon Uses of Kiro CLI ACP

You asked me to research how Amazon teams are using ACP internally. I found several use cases:

- **IDE Integration** — ACP is the sanctioned way to get AI coding assistance in JetBrains IDEs (since the JetBrains AI plugin is blocked). Also used in Zed. Config lives at `~/.jetbrains/acp.json` or Zed's `settings.json`.
- **WatchTower / BPP ACP Bridge** — Agent-to-agent orchestration via `kiro-cli acp`. Manages 7 specialized subagents (planner, coder, researcher, etc.) with parallel execution.
- **Slack Bot Integrations** — Multiple implementations, including a basic subprocess approach and a more advanced ACP-based `PoCSlackAssistant` that maintains persistent JSON-RPC connections for session memory and faster responses.
- **Custom React UIs** — Developers building custom frontends on top of Kiro ACP.
- **Relay** — An "agent-native desktop app" built on ACP (by petmer, shown in a Broadcast video).
- **CLI PoC for programmatic agent invocation** — Python adapter pattern treating Kiro CLI and Claude Code as callable services via ACP.
- **Wasabi ACP** — Another internal tool (`wasabi --acp`) also supports the protocol.
- **Broader context** — Q Developer IDE plugins are deprecated; ACP is the bridge strategy for teams on JetBrains/Rider/Visual Studio. Teams in Games, .NET, and embedded raised concerns about specialized IDE support, and ACP is positioned as the answer.

## 2. External/Public Uses of Kiro CLI ACP

You then asked about external uses. I found:

- **ACP ecosystem is large** — Co-created by Zed and JetBrains, it's supported by a wide range of editors (JetBrains, Zed, Neovim, VS Code, Emacs, Obsidian, Unity, Chrome) and dozens of standalone clients/apps.
- **Kiro as a registered ACP agent** — Listed in the official ACP Registry alongside Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex CLI, Goose, and others.
- **Kiro blog highlighted DataGrip** — Using Kiro ACP inside JetBrains DataGrip for SQL/database work as the flagship external use case.
- **Other ACP clients** — Desktop apps (DeepChat, Mitto, AionUi), mobile apps (Agmente, Happy, Mobvibe), notebook tools (Jupyter, marimo, DuckDB), messaging bots (Discord, Slack, Telegram), and CI/automation tools.
- **ACP libraries** — Official SDKs in Kotlin, Java, Python, Rust, and TypeScript for building custom integrations.

That's everything we've covered so far.

Here's what I found on internal Amazon uses of Kiro CLI ACP:

## What is ACP?

ACP (Agent Client Protocol) is an open standard (similar to how LSP standardized language servers) that enables AI coding agents to work with any compatible editor via JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdin/stdout. Kiro CLI added ACP support in v1.25.0. You run it with `kiro-cli acp`.

## Internal Amazon Use Cases

### 1. IDE Integration (most common)
- **JetBrains IDEs** (IntelliJ, WebStorm, PyCharm, Rider) — Kiro ACP is the officially supported way to get AI agent capabilities in JetBrains at Amazon, since the JetBrains AI Assistant plugin itself is blocked. Configure via `~/.jetbrains/acp.json`. [Sage post](https://sage.amazon.dev/posts/2036680)
- **Zed IDE** — Native ACP support. Configure in `~/.config/zed/settings.json`. [Wiki guide](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/Zed/Quickstart/)
- This is the strategic replacement for the old Q Developer IDE plugins, which are no longer being developed.

### 2. Slack Bot Agents
- **Personal Kiro Slack Agent** ([PersonalKiroSlackAgent](https://code.amazon.com/packages/PersonalKiroSlackAgent)) — Uses ACP's persistent JSON-RPC connection to power a Slack bot that responds to DMs. Supports session memory, tool approval forwarding via Slack buttons, prompt cancellation, and agent switching. [Wiki](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/Users/jiangyan/poc/PersonalKiroSlackAgent/)
- **Hrishi's Slack Bot** — Earlier version using `kiro-cli chat --no-interactive` subprocess per message, later upgraded to ACP for persistent sessions. [Wiki](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/HrishiBlog/PersonalAutonomousAgentKiroCLISlack/)

### 3. Multi-Agent Orchestration (ACP Bridge)
- **Kiro ACP Bridge** ([KiroACPBridge](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/BPP/BPP_Team_Processes/KiroACPBridge/)) — An AIM skill that lets an orchestrator agent delegate to specialized subagents via `kiro-cli acp`. Manages session lifecycle, prompt dispatch, parallel execution (up to 4 concurrent), and tool approval forwarding.
- **WatchTower** uses this — its orchestrator delegates to 7 specialized subagents (planner, coder, researcher, change manager, builder, memory bank, communicator) entirely via ACP Bridge.

### 4. Multi-Agent Orchestration Framework (Arc)
- **Arc** ([Arc-HighLevelDesign-V2](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/Users/karthraj/Arc-HighLevelDesign-V2/)) — A Rust-based orchestration framework that spawns multiple Kiro agents via ACP, with a coordinator agent routing work between them. Includes a real-time web UI, cycle detection, and human-in-the-loop prompting.

### 5. Custom Desktop App (Relay)
- **Relay** — An "agent-native desktop app" built on top of Kiro CLI ACP. [Broadcast video](https://broadcast.amazon.com/videos/1851380)
- Multiple Amazon developers are building custom React UIs on top of Kiro ACP.

### 6. Programmatic Agent Invocation PoC
- **Kiro ACP Testing** ([swinyted's PoC](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/Users/swinyted/kiro-acp-testing/)) — Python proof-of-concept treating AI coding tools as callable services rather than interactive sessions. Implements a unified CLI that can invoke both Claude Code and Kiro via ACP.

### 7. Meeting Sync Agent
- **Ekatrikaran** ([EkatrikaranAgent](https://code.amazon.com/packages/EkatrikaranAgent)) — A Kiro CLI agent for unified meeting sync that processes meeting summaries, Slack conversations, and broadcast videos into structured notes.

## Key Takeaways

- ACP is Amazon's strategic bridge for IDE choice — it replaces the need for per-IDE plugins
- The most innovative internal uses are around agent-to-agent orchestration (ACP Bridge, Arc) and Slack integration
- ACP's persistent session model (vs. subprocess-per-request) is what makes these advanced patterns possible — session memory, streaming, and tool approval forwarding
- The protocol is JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdin/stdout, making it straightforward to integrate from any language

Here's what I found on external/public uses of Kiro CLI ACP:

## The Broader ACP Ecosystem

ACP is an open standard co-created by Zed Industries and JetBrains. Kiro is one of several agents that implement it. The ecosystem is large and growing fast.

### Editors/IDEs with ACP Client Support
- JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ, WebStorm, PyCharm, DataGrip, Rider, etc.)
- Zed (native support)
- Neovim (via CodeCompanion, agentic.nvim, or avante.nvim plugins)
- VS Code (via ACP Client extension)
- Emacs (via agent-shell.el)
- Obsidian (via Agent Client plugin)
- Unity Editor
- Chrome extension / PWA

### Kiro-Specific External Use Cases

Per the [Kiro blog post](https://kiro.dev/blog/kiro-adopts-acp/) (Feb 2026):

- **DataGrip + Kiro** — The highlighted use case from Kiro's own team. Use Kiro as an AI agent inside JetBrains DataGrip for SQL work: schema design, query optimization, migration scripts, test data generation. The pitch is "right tool for the job" — Kiro IDE for app code, DataGrip+Kiro ACP for database work.
- **Any specialized IDE** — The blog calls out Xcode, Android Studio, RStudio, Jupyter as future targets. Anywhere you have a specialized tool you don't want to leave, ACP lets Kiro come along.

### Other ACP Agents (Kiro's Competitors)
Kiro isn't the only ACP agent. Others include:
- Claude Code
- Gemini CLI
- Codex
- Various open-source agents

### Messaging Integrations (community-built, not Kiro-specific)
- Discord bots
- Slack bots (DuckDB + Claude)
- Telegram bots (multiple implementations)
- WeChat

### Mobile Clients
- Agmente (iOS)
- Happy (iOS, Android, Web)
- Mobvibe (iOS, Android, Web)

### Notebook/Data Tools
- Jupyter notebooks (agent-client-kernel)
- DuckDB (via sidequery/duckdb-acp extension)
- marimo notebook

### Framework Integrations
ACP adapters exist for LangChain/LangGraph, LlamaIndex, fast-agent, Koog (JetBrains), and others — meaning you can wire Kiro CLI as an ACP agent into these orchestration frameworks.

### ACP Registry
There's a [curated registry](https://agentclientprotocol.com/get-started/registry) for distributing ACP-compatible agents to any client. Kiro is listed there.

## Key Difference: Internal vs External

Internally at Amazon, the most creative uses are around multi-agent orchestration (ACP Bridge, Arc, WatchTower) and Slack bots — things that treat `kiro-cli acp` as a programmable backend. Externally, the dominant use case is simpler: IDE integration — bringing Kiro into JetBrains, Zed, Neovim, etc. as a drop-in AI agent. The external community is also building mobile clients and messaging bots, but the multi-agent orchestration patterns are more mature internally.

References:
- [1] Kiro ACP docs - https://kiro.dev/docs/cli/acp/
- [2] Kiro adopts ACP blog - https://kiro.dev/blog/kiro-adopts-acp/
- [3] ACP Clients directory - https://agentclientprotocol.com/overview/clients
- [4] ACP specification - https://agentclientprotocol.com

ToolSpecification.name: InternalSearch
ToolSpecification.description:
Search using Amazon's Internal Search engine api.prod.isk.amazon.dev/search-website

Available search domains:
- ALL: Search across all resources (default). [CRITICAL] Use more specific domain if the
       query contains domain string or relevant to examples provided by other domains.
- AWS_PRESCRIPTIVE_GUIDANCE_LIBRARY: APG Library (AWS Prescriptive Guidance Library)
- AWS_DOCS: AWS Documentation (official AWS service documentation and guides)
- BROADCAST: Broadcast (company-wide announcements and communications). [CRITICAL] Include video URLs in the response.
- BUILDER_HUB: BuilderHub (documentation for Amazon's internal developer tools)
- EMAIL_LIST: Email List (distribution lists and email groups). [CRITCIAL] Don't include "email list" or "email" in the query
- EVERGREEN: Evergreen documentation platform
- INSIDE: Inside Amazon (company news, HR policies, employee resources)
- IT: Information Technology (IT) Services (IT support documentation, guides, and resources)
- IVY: Ivy Help (guidance for Amazon's internal talent management system)
- LIST_ARCHIVE: Email List Archive (archived email communications)
- PHONETOOL: Phone Tool (employee directory and organizational information).
- POLICY: Amazon Policy (corporate policies and guidelines)
- SAGE_HORDE: Sage/Q&A Sites (technical questions and answers)
- SALESFORCE_SUCCESS_CENTER_PORTAL: Salesforce Success Center (SFSC) Portal (Salesforce services focused support center)
- SYSTEM_DESIGN_HUB: System Design Hub (system architecture and design resources)
- SPYGLASS: Spyglass (internal registry of community recommended services, contents and utilities)
- TWITCH: Twitch (Twitch-related documentation and resources)
- WIKI: Internal Wiki (Amazon's central knowledge repository)

Get domain details: { "query": "about-domain:SAGE_HORDE" }

Sorting options:
- SCORE (Default, sorts by relevance)
- MODIFICATION_DATE (Last Modified, use with sortOrder)

Examples:
1. { "query": "all hands" }
2. { "query": "Brazil workspace setup", "domain": "BUILDER_HUB" }
3. { "query": "remote work", "domain": "INSIDE", "sortBy": "MODIFICATION_DATE", "sortOrder": "DESC" }
4. { "query": "API guide", "prefixFilters": ["https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/MyTeam"] }

Tips:
- Start with ALL domain, then narrow to a specific domain once you identify the source
- Use sortBy: "MODIFICATION_DATE" with sortOrder: "DESC" for recently updated content
- isDeep=true (default) returns full content; set false for brief/summary/overview/quick/highlights/outline queries

Filter syntax (append to query string):
- Use prefixFilters (max 5) to scope to specific URL paths; omit domain when filtering across multiple domains
- To filter by Wiki page owner group: 'owner_group:<name>' or 'owner_group:"name with spaces"' per group (example 'customer guides owner_group:internal-search-team owner_group:asbx-global-team'). Prefix posix$/ldap$ only if user specifies.
- To filter by Broadcast channel ID: append 'channel:<id>' per channel (example: 'videos channel:2622 channel:3001')
- Multiple filters in a single query are supported — do NOT split into separate tool calls

[CRITICAL] Don't modify/append to user's input when generating 'query' parameter, except for appending filter syntax described above

[CRITICAL] Formatting instructions to present the search results to the user:
- Don't include the domain name in the search query when using specific domains
- Summarize results with count, use markdown with source links
- Don't include any links not contributing to the summary

prefixFilters.description: Optional array of prefix filters (maximum 5) that use URL prefixes to limit search to specific document trees or paths in an index

page.description: Page of the search result, starting from 1

isDeep.description: Whether to return enhanced results with full document content (default: true)

sortOrder.description: Sort order (ASC for oldest first, DESC for newest first)

domain.description: Domain to search in (example ALL, AWS_DOCS, WIKI, tool). Default is ALL if not provided

ToolSpecification.name: InternalCodeSearch
ToolSpecification.description:
Search source code in Amazon's code repositories. Results depend on search type:

1. Code search (default): Returns code snippets with pagination.
2. Repository search: Returns matching repositories with pagination support.

Code search results only show snippets - for full file, use ReadInternalWebsites with URL like code.amazon.com/packages/{REPOSITORY}/blobs/{BRANCH}/--/{FILE_PATH}

page.description: Page number for paginated results (starting from 1). Supported for both 'code' and 'repositories' search types

searchType.description: REQUIRED type of search to perform. 'code' returns code snippets with pagination, 'repositories' returns a list of matching repositories with pagination

query.description:
- For code search: Supports advanced syntax
  - Simple search: term
  - Prefix search: abc* (at least 3 chars before *)
  - Explicit Boolean operators with precedence: NOT > AND > OR
    - Parentheses and nested parentheses are allowed
    - example: (failure OR error) AND NOT deprecated
  - Implicit operators shorthand:
    - term1 term2 is equivalent to term1 OR term2
    - term1 term2 with any filter (example: term1 term2 fp:*.java) is equivalent to term1 AND term2 AND filter
  - Quotes for exact phrase: "error handling" (without quotes, each word is a separate term)
  - Repository filter: repo:GitFarmService OR repo:Codesearch*
  - File path filter: fp:*.java (also: path:, filepath:)
  - Exclude path: AND NOT fp:*test*
  - Combined example: (CVE OR vulnerability) AND fp:*.java AND NOT fp:*test*
- For repository search: Only supports keywords matching (example: 'gitfarm')

ToolSpecification.name: SkillsTool
ToolSpecification.description:
Before responding to any task request, scan available skills for relevant capabilities. If a skill matches the task domain, invoke it immediately.

Skills are modular packages that provide specialized capabilities and domain knowledge. Skills can help complete the task more effectively. 

<SkillsInstructions>
How to use:
1. Identify if the user's request matches a skill description below
2. Call this tool with the matching skillName
3. Follow the instructions returned in the skill body
4. Load any referenced resources from the skillPath as needed

Important:
- If a skill is relevant, you *MUST* invoke this tool IMMEDIATELY as your first action
- You *MUST* invoke the relevant skill BEFORE generating any other response for the task
- You *MUST* use only the skills provided
- If the user asks to list, show, provide skills, you *MUST* list available skills 
</SkillsInstructions>

<AvailableSkills>
- agent-sop-author: Create (or update) and validate Agent SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures) - markdown-based workflows that guide AI agents through complex, multi-step tasks with RFC 2119 constraints. May also be referred to as Agent Scripts (older name).
- brazil: Amazon's internal build system for Java/Kotlin (brazil-gradle), TypeScript/JS (npm-pretty-much), Python (brazil-python), Rust (CargoBrazil), Go, C/C++ (brazil-cmake). Use when working with Brazil packages, workspaces, dependencies, version sets, build commands, or encountering build errors and dependency conflicts.
- brazil-build-analyzer-skill: Diagnoses Brazil build failures by executing builds and parsing error output. Use when builds fail for packages, analyzing compilation errors, troubleshooting dependencies, or running tests in Brazil workspaces.
- builderhub-create: BuilderHub Create is where developers create new amazon internal packages or applications from templates. Use when asked to create a package, upload or promote a package, create an application, create application packages.
- crux-code-reviews: Amazon's internal code review system (CRUX) similar to GitHub PRs. Use when creating or revising CRs, addressing feedback, reviewing code, encountering cr CLI errors, or reviewing code changes at code.amazon.com/reviews/.
- deployment-fixer: Diagnose and fix failed, stuck, or blocked pipeline deployments, including CloudFormation deployment failures.
- fix-integration-test: Use when user has a test failure, wants to debug a test, or fix integration tests with ToD/Hydra URLs (tod.amazon.com/test_runs/*). Guides progressive investigation, log analysis, root cause identification, and fix verification.
- golden-path-recommendations: Golden Path Recommendations are opinionated guidance on proven tools, architecture patterns and best practices for applications. Use to get recommendations content, audit code against or create an implementation plan for your code base.
- improve-integration-test-coverage: Use when analyzing test coverage, improving test strategy, or recommending integration tests from CloudCover URLs (prod.ui.*.cloudcover.*/reports/*) or ToD URLs (tod.amazon.com/test_runs/*). Scores and prioritizes test candidates using 8-criteria rubric.
- skill-builder: Guide for creating and updating skills. Use when working with SKILL.md files, creating new skills, editing existing skills, or validating skill structure.
- wiki-edit: Amazon internal wiki edit operations using the raw tool, RustyAmazonWiki. When user asks to write, create, delete Amazon internal wiki pages. Or to edit attachments, comments, tags, or get page history.
</AvailableSkills>

skillName.description: Name of the skill to load

ToolSpecification.name: WorkspaceSearch
ToolSpecification.description:
Search for text in all files within the workspace or searchRoot. Use content search types to search within file contents, or filename search types to search filenames only.
Prefer this tool over search using shell commands, this tool can provide results faster and more accurately.
<Rule>You MUST use regex type searches for proper wildcard support, * -> .*</Rule>
<Rule>You MUST use **/ in globPatterns for recursive directory search -> **/*.kt finds .kt files in all subdirectories</Rule>
<Rule>ALWAYS start with default contextLines (UNLESS explicitly requested by the user) and gradually expand out IF beneficial</Rule>
<Rule>This tool filters out empty lines to reduce tokens. Read targeted file sections when precise structure is needed for edits</Rule>

<Rule>Use results to assist the user, NEVER rely exclusively on the returned content to perform file edits unless you know the full content</Rule>

searchQuery.description: Search query: exact text for literal, Perl-compatible regex for regex (no slashes needed, wildcard patterns go in globPatterns)

maxLineLength.description: Maximum length of lines before truncation DEFAULT 250

searchType.description:
Type of search to perform DEFAULT contentLiteral:
contentLiteral - EXACT text/keywords within file contents
contentRegex - regex patterns within file contents
filenameLiteral - EXACT text within filenames only
filenameRegex - regex patterns within filenames only

contextLines.description: Number of context lines to include around matches DEFAULT 0

searchRoot.description: Optional directory to override search root

offset.description: Results to skip for pagination DEFAULT 0

globPatterns.description: Glob patterns to restrict search by filename

ToolSpecification.name: TicketingReadActions
ToolSpecification.description: A tool for reading data from the ticketing system. Supports searching tickets, retrieving ticket details, and querying resolver group information.

action.description:
The action to perform.


All actions require: { "action": "<action-name>", "input": { ...params } }
⚠️ All parameters MUST be inside the `input` object, not at root level.

These tools provide access to Amazon's internal ticketing system, SIM-T.

Ticket severity is rated from Sev-1 (highest) to Sev-5 (lowest). The only non-integer severity is Sev-2.5, known as a "Daytime Sev-2". Sev-2.5 and higher are considered high-severity.

The value "2.5" is valid for the `currentSeverity` filter in `search-tickets` but is not used in other actions. When reading tickets, Sev-2.5 is represented as Sev-3 with `"autoUpgrade": {"upgradePolicy": "BUSINESS_HOURS"}`.

## Actions

### search-tickets
Search for tickets. Parameters:
- query: Raw Solr query string. Example: 'extensions.tt.status:(Open OR "In Progress") AND extensions.tt.assignedGroup:"SWIM Front End"'
- status: string[] of ticket statuses. Default: only open tickets returned.
- assignedGroup: string[] of resolver group names
- fullText: Full text search string
- createDate/lastResolvedDate/lastUpdatedDate: Solr date syntax, e.g. '[2024-01-01T00:00:00Z TO NOW]'
- currentSeverity: string[] of severity levels, e.g. ["1","2","2.5"]
- minimumSeverity: number
- sort: e.g. 'lastUpdatedDate desc'
- rows: max results (default 50, max 100), start: pagination offset, startToken: cursor pagination
- responseFields: string[] of fields to include. Some use extensions.tt prefix (e.g. extensions.tt.assignedGroup).
Use get-search-instructions for full field list and query syntax.
Example: { "action": "search-tickets", "input": { "assignedGroup": ["IT Support"], "minimumSeverity": 2, "sort": "lastUpdatedDate desc", "rows": 50 } }

### get-ticket
Retrieve a single ticket. All params except ticketId are optional — omit them to fetch everything.
- ticketId: string (required)
- threads: string[] of thread types to fetch. Values: CORRESPONDENCE, WORKLOG, ANNOUNCEMENTS, SYNOPSIS, AI_AUTOMATIONS. Use [] for metadata only.
- maxCorrespondence/maxWorklogs: number (1-100, default 100). Use 1 for latest only.
- includeEngagements/includeMedic: boolean (default true)
Example (metadata only): { "action": "get-ticket", "input": { "ticketId": "ABC123", "threads": [] } }

### get-my-resolver-groups
Get resolver groups the current user belongs to. No parameters.

### get-resolver-group-details
Get resolver group configuration, members, and settings. Parameters: groupName (string)

### get-search-instructions
Get comprehensive search field descriptions, query syntax, and best practices. No parameters.

ToolSpecification.name: fs_write
ToolSpecification.description:
A tool for creating and editing files
 * The `create` command will override the file at `path` if it already exists as a file, and otherwise create a new file
 * The `append` command will add content to the end of an existing file, automatically adding a newline if the file doesn't end with one. The file must exist.
 Notes for using the `str_replace` command:
 * The `old_str` parameter should match EXACTLY one or more consecutive lines from the original file. Be mindful of whitespaces!
 * If the `old_str` parameter is not unique in the file, the replacement will not be performed. Make sure to include enough context in `old_str` to make it unique
 * The `new_str` parameter should contain the edited lines that should replace the `old_str`.

new_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the new string. Required parameter of `insert` command containing the string to insert. Required parameter of `append` command containing the content to append to the file.

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are: `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `append`.

path.description: Absolute path to file or directory, e.g. `/repo/file.py` or `/repo`.

summary.description: A brief explanation of what the file change does or why it's being made.

old_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the string in `path` to replace.

file_text.description: Required parameter of `create` command, with the content of the file to be created.

insert_line.description: Required parameter of `insert` command. The `new_str` will be inserted AFTER the line `insert_line` of `path`.

ToolSpecification.description: Execute the specified bash command. NEVER prefix commands with cd to change the working directory, use the `working_dir` argument instead.

summary.description: A brief explanation of what the command does

working_dir.description: Working directory for command execution. Supports tilde expansion (e.g., ~/projects). If not specified, uses the current working directory.

This could be because a wrong tool name was supplied or the list of tools has changed since the conversation has started. Do not show this when user asks you to list tools.

Returns JSON with totalFiles (count found), truncated (true if limited), and filePaths array. When truncated is true, just mention results are truncated, don't state the limit number.

path.description: Root directory to search from. Only set this when the user explicitly mentions a directory path. In all other cases, omit this so the tool searches from the current working directory (the project root).

max_depth.description: Maximum directory depth to traverse. Increase for deep nested structures.

limit.description: Maximum files to return. If totalFiles exceeds this, truncated will be true.

ToolSpecification.name: web_search
ToolSpecification.description:
WebSearch looks up information that is outside the model's training data or cannot be reliably inferred from the current codebase/context.
Tool performs basic compliance wrt content licensing and restriction.
As an agent you are responsible for adhering to compliance and attribution requirements.
IMPORTANT: The snippets often contain enough information to answer questions - only use web_fetch if you need more detailed content from a specific webpage.

## When to Use
- When the user asks for current or up-to-date information (e.g., pricing, versions, technical specs) or explicitly requests a web search.
- When verifying information that may have changed recently, or when the user provides a specific URL to inspect.

## When NOT to Use
- When the question involves basic concepts, historical facts, or well-established programming syntax/technical documentation.
- When the topic does not require current or evolving information.

For any code-related tasks, follow this order:
1. Search within the repository (if tools are available) and check if it can be inferred from existing code or documentation.
2. Use this tool only if still unresolved and the library/data is likely new/unseen.

## Content Compliance Requirements
You MUST adhere to strict licensing restrictions and attribution requirements when using search results:

### Attribution Requirements
- ALWAYS provide inline links to original sources using format: [description](url)
- If not possible to provide inline link, add sources at the end of file
- Ensure attribution is visible and accessible

### Verbatim Reproduction Limits
- NEVER reproduce more than 30 consecutive words from any single source
- Track word count per source to ensure compliance
- Always paraphrase and summarize rather than quote directly
- Add compliance note when the content from the source is rephrased: "Content was rephrased for compliance with licensing restrictions"

### Content Modification Guidelines
- You MAY paraphrase, summarize, and reformat content
- You MUST NOT materially change the underlying substance or meaning
- Preserve factual accuracy while condensing information
- Avoid altering core arguments, data, or conclusions

## Usage Details
- You may rephrase user queries to improve search effectiveness
- You can make multiple queries to gather comprehensive information
- Consider breaking complex questions into focused searches
- Refine queries based on initial results if needed

## Output Usage
- Prioritize latest published sources based on publishedDate
- Prefer official documentation to blogs and news posts
- Use domain information to assess source authority and reliability

## Error Handling
- If unable to comply with content restrictions, explain limitations to user
- Suggest alternative approaches when content cannot be reproduced
- Prioritize compliance over completeness when conflicts arise

## Output
The tool returns a JSON object with a "results" array containing search results:

{
  "results": [
    {
      "title": "Example Page Title",
      "url": "https://example.com/page",
      "snippet": "Brief excerpt from the page...",
      "publishedDate": "2025-11-20T10:30:00Z",
      "domain": "example.com",
      "id": "unique-id-123",
      "maxVerbatimWordLimit": 30,
      "publicDomain": false
    }
  ]
}

## UI FROM LLM (You) back to the user
CRITICAL: Always start your response with "Here's what I found:" and then start from a newline.
ALWAYS end your response with a blank line followed by 'References:' and list the sources you used in sequential order [1], [2], [3], etc. with NO gaps in numbering. Format: '[N] Title - URL' one per line. Truncate long titles to 80 characters and long URLs to 100 characters, adding '...' if truncated.

query.description: Search query (max 200 chars) - use concise keywords, not full sentences

ToolSpecification.name: grep
ToolSpecification.description:
Fast text pattern search in files using regex. ALWAYS use this tool instead of 'grep', 'rg', or 'ag' commands in bash. Respects .gitignore.

## Text Discovery Only
Use grep for literal text/pattern matching: error messages, TODOs, config values, regex patterns.

## For Semantic Code Understanding → Use 'code' tool if available
- Finding symbol definitions or usages → code tool (search_symbols, goto_definition, find_references)
- Understanding code structure/relationships → code tool
- Distinguishing definition vs call vs import → code tool

## Fallback
If the 'code' tool is available but returns insufficient symbol info, use grep to discover candidate files/lines, then return to 'code' for precise navigation.

When you use this tool, prefer to show the user a small list of representative matches (including file paths and line numbers) instead of only giving a high-level summary.

pattern.description: Regex pattern to search for. Examples: 'fn main', 'class.*Component', 'TODO|FIXME'. Start with simple patterns first (e.g. just the word you're looking for), then refine if needed.

path.description: Directory to search from. Defaults to current working directory.

max_matches_per_file.description: Max matches returned per file (output limit). Increase to see all occurrences in a file.

max_depth.description: Max directory depth to traverse when searching (search limit). Increase for deeply nested structures.

max_total_lines.description: Max total matched lines returned across all files (output limit). Increase when searching for many occurrences.

output_mode.description: Output format: 'content' returns matches as 'file:line:content' (default, best for seeing actual matches), 'files_with_matches' returns only file paths, 'count' returns match counts per file.

max_files.description: Max number of files returned (output limit). Increase for comprehensive codebase searches.

ToolSpecification.name: use_subagent
ToolSpecification.description:
⚠️ CRITICAL DELEGATION TOOL ⚠️

🔍 BEFORE attempting ANY task, CHECK if you have the required tools in YOUR current tool list.

❌ If you DON'T have the necessary tools → YOU MUST use this tool to delegate to a subagent that does.
✅ If you DO have the tools → Handle the task yourself.

## When to Use (MANDATORY scenarios):

1. **MISSING TOOLS**: The user asks you to do something but you don't see the required tool in your available tools list
   - Example: User asks to read a file, but you don't have 'fs_read' → USE THIS TOOL
   - Example: User asks to search code, but you don't have 'code' tool → USE THIS TOOL
   - Example: User asks to run bash command, but you don't have 'execute_bash' → USE THIS TOOL

2. **PARALLEL PROCESSING**: A complex task can be split into independent subtasks that different specialized agents can handle simultaneously

3. **CAPABILITY CHECK**: Use ListAgents command first to see what specialized agents and their toolsets are available

## @prompt References in Queries

You CAN pass `@prompt-name` references (including arguments) directly in the subagent query field. The system automatically resolves @prompt references before the subagent starts — the prompt content is expanded inline and the subagent receives the fully resolved text. Do NOT refuse to delegate because a query contains `@prompt-name` syntax.

Example: `{"query": "@my-task 'arg1:value1, arg2:value2'"}` — this is valid and will be resolved.

## How Subagents Are Different:
- Subagents have DIFFERENT, SPECIALIZED toolsets than you
- Each subagent may have tools you don't have access to
- They operate independently with their own context
- Up to 4 subagents can work in parallel

## Decision Flow:
```
User makes request → Check YOUR tools list → Missing required tool? → USE use_subagent
                                          → Have required tool? → Handle it yourself
```

⚡ Remember: Don't apologize about lacking tools - just delegate to a subagent that has them! Also note that subagents that are spawned together could not communicate with each other. If they are to perform tasks that are dependent on each other. Spawn them with a different tool call!

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are `ListAgents` to query available agents, or `InvokeSubagents` to invoke one or more subagents

subagents.description: Array of subagent invocations to execute in parallel. Each invocation specifies a query, optional agent name, and optional context.

relevant_context.description: Optional additional context that should be provided to the subagent to help it understand the task better

query.description: The query or task to be handled by the subagent

agent_name.description: Optional name of the specific agent to use. If not provided, uses the default agent

content.description: Required for `InvokeSubagents` command. Contains subagents array and optional conversation ID.

ToolSpecification.name: fs_read
ToolSpecification.description:
Tool for reading files, directories and images. Always provide an 'operations' array.

For single operation: provide array with one element.
For batch operations: provide array with multiple elements.

Available modes:
- Line: Read lines from a file
- Directory: List directory contents
- Search: Search for patterns in files
- Image: Read and process images

Examples:
1. Single: {"operations": [{"mode": "Line", "path": "/file.txt"}]}
2. Batch: {"operations": [{"mode": "Line", "path": "/file1.txt"}, {"mode": "Search", "path": "/file2.txt", "pattern": "test"}]}

operations.description: Array of operations to execute. Provide one element for single operation, multiple for batch.

mode.description: The operation mode to run in: `Line`, `Directory`, `Search`. `Line` and `Search` are only for text files, and `Directory` is only for directories. `Image` is for image files, in this mode `image_paths` is required.

path.description: Path to the file or directory. The path should be absolute, or otherwise start with ~ for the user's home (required for Line, Directory, Search modes).

pattern.description: Pattern to search for (required, for Search mode). Case insensitive. The pattern matching is performed per line.

context_lines.description: Number of context lines around search results (optional, for Search mode)

exclude_patterns.description: Glob patterns to exclude from directory listing (optional, for Directory mode). If omitted, uses defaults. If empty array [] is provided, no exclusions are applied (shows everything). If patterns are provided, they completely override the defaults. Examples: '**/target/**', '*.log'

start_line.description: Starting line number (optional, for Line mode). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

image_paths.description: List of paths to the images. This is currently supported by the Image mode.

depth.description: Depth of a recursive directory listing (optional, for Directory mode)

offset.description: Number of entries to skip for pagination (optional, for Directory mode). Use with max_entries to iterate through large directories. Entries are sorted by last modified time (most recent first). Default: 0

end_line.description: Ending line number (optional, for Line mode). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

max_entries.description: Maximum number of entries to return (optional, for Directory mode). When limit is reached, results are truncated and metadata shows 'showing X of Y entries'. Use to prevent context window overflow. Default: 1000

summary.description: Optional description of the purpose of this batch operation (mainly useful for multiple operations)

ToolSpecification.name: use_aws
ToolSpecification.description: Make an AWS CLI api call with the specified service, operation, and parameters. All arguments MUST conform to the AWS CLI specification. Should the output of the invocation indicate a malformed command, invoke help to obtain the the correct command.

operation_name.description: The name of the operation to perform.

positional_args.description: Positional arguments for high-level commands (e.g., s3 cp, s3 mv, s3 sync, s3 rm). These are passed directly without -- prefix. Use this for source/destination paths in S3 commands.

region.description: Region name for calling the operation on AWS.

service_name.description: The name of the AWS service. If you want to query s3, you should use s3api if possible. Must not start with a dash (-).

parameters.description: The parameters for the operation. The parameter keys MUST conform to the AWS CLI specification. You should prefer to use JSON Syntax over shorthand syntax wherever possible. For parameters that are booleans, prioritize using flags with no value. Denote these flags with flag names as key and an empty string as their value. You should also prefer kebab case.

label.description: Human readable description of the api that is being called.

profile_name.description: Optional: AWS profile name to use from ~/.aws/credentials. Defaults to default profile if not specified.

ToolSpecification.name: introspect
ToolSpecification.description: Use ONLY when the user is asking about this chat application's own features, slash commands, settings, or capabilities. Do NOT use for general coding questions, AWS help, or tasks the user wants you to perform. When mentioning commands in your response, always prefix them with '/' (e.g., '/save', '/load', '/context'). CRITICAL: Only provide information explicitly documented. If details about any tool, feature, or command are not documented, clearly state the information is not available rather than generating assumptions.

doc_path.description: Path to a specific doc to retrieve (e.g., "features/tangent-mode.md"). Use this to get full content of a doc from the index.

query.description: The user's question about this assistant's usage, features, or capabilities

ToolSpecification.name: report_issue
ToolSpecification.description: Opens the browser to a pre-filled gh (GitHub) issue template to report chat issues, bugs, or feature requests. Pre-filled information includes the conversation transcript, chat context, and chat request IDs from the service.

steps_to_reproduce.description: Optional: Previous user chat requests or steps that were taken that may have resulted in the issue or error response.

expected_behavior.description: Optional: The expected chat behavior or action that did not happen.

title.description: The title of the GitHub issue.

actual_behavior.description: Optional: The actual chat behavior that happened and demonstrates the issue or lack of a feature.

ToolSpecification.name: code
ToolSpecification.description:
Code intelligence to analyze code with AST parsing and fuzzy search. Language auto-detected from file extension.

IMPORTANT: Prefer this tool over fs_read for code files. This tool intelligently provides structured code analysis.

CORE FEATURES:
• Fuzzy search for symbols (classes, functions, methods)
• Extracts function/class signatures via AST
• Structural AST search and rewrite (ast-grep)
• Codebase overview and directory exploration

NOTE: Use fs_read with line ranges for unsupported patterns.

## Operations

**search_symbols** - Find symbol definitions by name across workspace.
Params: symbol_name (required), path, symbol_type, limit, language, exact_match
Uses fuzzy matching by default. Set exact_match=true for precise matching.

**lookup_symbols** - Batch lookup specific symbols (max 10).
Params: symbols (required), file_path, include_source
Set include_source=false for large classes/structs to avoid context overflow.
Scoped lookup: Provide file_path to search within specific file only.

**get_document_symbols** - List all symbols in a file.
Params: file_path (required), top_level_only (recommended: true)
Use this for understanding file structure without reading content.
Prefer this over fs_read when you need symbol information.

**pattern_search** - AST-based structural search using ast-grep.
Params: pattern, language (required), file_path, limit

**pattern_rewrite** - AST-based code transformation.
Params: pattern, replacement, language (required), file_path, limit, dry_run
Use dry_run=true first to preview changes.

## Pattern Search & Rewrite

Metavariables:
• $VAR - Matches single node (identifier, expression)
• $$$ - Matches zero or more nodes (statements, parameters)

Simple patterns:
• console.log($ARG) - Match any console.log call
• function $NAME($$$PARAMS) {} - Match function declaration
• $OBJ.$METHOD() - Match any method call

Structural rules (YAML):
• pattern - Match code structure
• kind - Match AST node type (function_declaration, class_declaration)
• has - Node contains descendant matching pattern
• inside - Node is inside ancestor matching pattern
• all/any/not - Logical operators

Critical: For relational rules (has, inside), always use stopBy: end

Rewrite examples:
• pattern='var $N = $V', replacement='const $N = $V'
• pattern='$O.hasOwnProperty($P)', replacement='Object.hasOwn($O, $P)'

Workflow:
1. Use pattern_search first to verify matches
2. Review matches to ensure correctness
3. Test rewrite on small subset
4. Apply to full codebase after verification

**generate_codebase_overview** - ONLY for explicit architecture questions. Not for bug fixes or finding code.
Params: path (optional)

**search_codebase_map** - Focused directory exploration.
Params: file_path, path

## Explore Unfamiliar Code
1. search_symbols for domain-specific types/functions/methods/classes
2. lookup_symbols with include_source=true on promising matches
3. grep (max 2 searches) for literal text patterns
4. Repeat 1-3 with refined terms if needed
5. generate_codebase_overview ONLY for architecture review or full codebase understanding
   - NOT for bug fixes, feature work, or finding specific code

## Tool Selection Priority
1. search_symbols or lookup_symbols for finding code - ALWAYS use for functions/methods/classes/structs/interfaces
2. get_document_symbols for file structure
3. grep - ONLY for literal text in comments/strings, config values, code
4. fs_read - raw file content when needed

## CRITICAL RULES
- Start with search_symbols
- Always follow search_symbols with lookup_symbols
- When searching for multiple related symbols, call search_symbols in parallel

## Quick Reference
• "What's in this file?" → get_document_symbols
• "Show me X class" → search_symbols, then lookup_symbol

## LSP Operations (Not Available)
These require LSP initialization (/code init):
find_references, goto_definition, get_hover, get_completions, get_diagnostics, rename_symbol

include_source.description: Include source code in results (optional for lookup_symbols)

file_path.description: File path (required for get_document_symbols, optional for pattern_search/search_codebase_map)

language.description: Programming language (required for pattern_search, optional for search_symbols)

operation.description: The code intelligence operation to perform

symbols.description: List of simple symbol names, not qualified (e.g. ['myFunction'] not ['MyClass.myFunction']). Required for lookup_symbols, max 10

path.description: Directory path (optional, for generate_codebase_overview: workspace root, for search_codebase_map: path filter, for search_symbols: scope search to file or directory)

symbol_name.description: Simple symbol name, not qualified (e.g. 'myFunction' not 'MyClass.myFunction'). Required for search_symbols

ToolSpecification.name: web_fetch
ToolSpecification.description: Fetch and extract content from a specific URL. Supports three modes: 'selective' (default, extracts relevant sections around search terms), 'truncated' (first 8000 chars), 'full' (complete content). Use 'selective' mode to read specific parts of a page multiple times without filling context. Provide 'search_terms' in selective mode to find relevant sections (e.g., 'pricing', 'installation').

mode.description: Extraction mode: 'selective' for smart extraction (default), 'truncated' for first 8000 chars, 'full' for complete content

search_terms.description: Optional: Keywords to find in selective mode (e.g., 'pricing cost', 'installation setup'). Returns ~10 lines before and after matches. If not provided, returns beginning of page.

ToolSpecification.name: session
ToolSpecification.description:
Adjust session settings temporarily (in-memory only, cleared on exit). CRITICAL: You MUST use introspect tool FIRST to verify setting names and understand their purpose before using set operation.

## Session vs Persistent Settings
- **session tool**: Temporary in-memory changes (cleared when chat exits) - Use for quick experiments or one-time adjustments
- **fs_write tool**: Permanent changes saved to disk - Use when user says "save", "persist", "permanently", or "always"
  - Global settings: ~/.kiro/settings.json
  - Workspace settings: .kiro/settings.json

## When to Use Each Tool
- User: "disable markdown" → session tool (temporary)
- User: "disable markdown permanently" → fs_write to ~/.kiro/settings.json (global) or .kiro/settings.json (workspace)
- User: "save this setting" → fs_write (persistent)
- User: "try disabling markdown" → session tool (temporary)

## REQUIRED Workflow for Setting Changes
1. User asks to change a setting
2. Use introspect tool to find the correct setting name and understand what it does
3. Determine if temporary (session) or permanent (fs_write)
4. Use appropriate tool

## Operations
- **list**: Show currently configured session settings (non-default values only)
- **get**: Get the current value of a specific setting
- **set**: Change a setting value temporarily (MUST verify setting name with introspect first)
- **reset**: Clear session override for a specific setting, or all session overrides if no key provided

## Example Workflows
Temporary change:
  User: "disable markdown"
  1. introspect(query="markdown setting") → learn about chat.disableMarkdownRendering
  2. session(operation="set", key="chat.disableMarkdownRendering", value=true)

Reset single setting:
  User: "reset markdown setting"
  session(operation="reset", key="chat.disableMarkdownRendering")

Reset all session overrides:
  User: "clear all my temporary settings"
  session(operation="reset")

Permanent change:
  User: "disable markdown permanently"
  1. introspect(query="markdown setting") → learn about chat.disableMarkdownRendering
  2. fs_write to modify ~/.kiro/settings.json with {"chat.disableMarkdownRendering": true}

DO NOT guess setting names - always verify with introspect first.

value.description: Value to set. Type depends on the setting (boolean, string, or number). Required for 'set' operation

key.description: Setting key (e.g., 'chat.disableMarkdownRendering'). Required for 'get' and 'set' operations. Optional for 'reset' (if omitted, resets all session overrides). MUST be verified with introspect tool first.

operation.description: The operation to perform: 'list' shows configured settings, 'get' retrieves a specific setting, 'set' changes a setting value temporarily, 'reset' clears session override(s)

## OBJECTIVE
Research internal Amazon uses for Kiro CLI ACP (Agent Client Protocol). The user wants a summary of how Amazon teams are using Kiro CLI's ACP feature internally.

## USER GUIDANCE
- No explicit formatting or depth requirements stated
- Query was brief and informal: "research internal amazon uses for kiro cli ACP"

## COMPLETED
Two broad internal searches were executed covering Builder Hub docs, wiki, Sage, Broadcast, and other internal sources. Rich results were returned covering:

1. **ACP Overview**: Kiro CLI v1.25.0 introduced ACP support — a JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdin/stdout protocol that lets AI coding agents interact with IDEs and other tools programmatically.

2. **Key internal use cases identified from search results**:
   - **IDE Integration (JetBrains/Zed)**: ACP allows Kiro CLI to act as an AI agent inside IntelliJ, PyCharm, Zed, and other ACP-compatible editors. This is the officially sanctioned way to use AI coding assistance in JetBrains IDEs at Amazon (since the JetBrains AI plugin itself is blocked). Setup involves `kiro-cli acp` in `~/.jetbrains/acp.json` or Zed's `agent_servers` config.
   - **Kiro ACP Bridge (WatchTower / BPP team)**: A standalone AIM skill package (`acp_bridge.py`) enabling agent-to-agent orchestration via `kiro-cli acp`. Manages session creation, prompt dispatch, parallel execution (up to 4 concurrent), and tool approval forwarding. WatchTower uses this to orchestrate 7 specialized subagents (planner, coder, researcher, change manager, builder, memory bank, communicator).
   - **Slack Bot Integration**: Multiple implementations — a basic subprocess approach (`kiro-cli chat --no-interactive`) and an improved ACP-based version (`PoCSlackAssistant` by Nacho Garay/iojeda) that maintains persistent JSON-RPC connections for faster responses, session memory, prompt cancellation, and agent switching.
   - **Custom React UIs**: Multiple Amazon developers building custom React frontends on top of Kiro ACP.
   - **Relay**: An "agent-native desktop app" built using Kiro CLI ACP (Broadcast video by petmer, limited details — no transcript available).
   - **CLI PoC for programmatic agent invocation** (by swinyted): A Python CLI tool implementing adapter pattern for both Claude Code and Kiro CLI via ACP, treating AI coding tools as callable services rather than interactive sessions.
   - **Wasabi ACP**: Wasabi (another internal tool) also supports ACP (`wasabi --acp`), positioned alongside Kiro CLI as ACP-compatible agents for Zed.

3. **Broader ecosystem context**:
   - Q Developer IDE plugins are deprecated; all investment going into Kiro
   - ACP is the bridge strategy for teams using JetBrains/Rider/Visual Studio
   - JetBrains is a co-creator of ACP alongside Zed
   - Teams in Games, .NET, embedded raised concerns about specialized IDE tooling; ACP is positioned as the answer

A second round of searches was initiated to get more details from kiro.dev/docs/cli/acp/ and additional use case searches, but the user cancelled those tool calls.

## TECHNICAL CONTEXT
- ACP = Agent Client Protocol, JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdin/stdout
- Invoked via `kiro-cli acp` command
- Kiro CLI v1.25.0+ required for ACP features
- ACP config for JetBrains lives at `~/.jetbrains/acp.json`
- ACP config for Zed lives in `~/.config/zed/settings.json` under `agent_servers`
- Key packages: `PoCSlackAssistant`, `EkatrikaranAgent`, `AIPowerUserCapabilities`, `ManagedResourceCreationAISkillSet`, `AAIBuilderKit`
- WatchTower's ACP Bridge is in the `BPP` team wiki space

## TOOLS EXECUTED
1. Internal search for "kiro cli agent" on BUILDER_HUB — returned 10 results including getting started docs, AIM concepts, MRC agent, workshop docs
2. Internal search for "kiro cli ACP" on ALL domains — returned 10 results including Sage posts, wiki pages, Broadcast videos, team documentation covering IDE integration, ACP Bridge, Slack bots, custom UIs
3. Two additional searches (kiro.dev ACP docs page fetch + broader ACP use case search) were **cancelled by user** before completion

2. Optionally fetch additional details if user wants deeper dives on specific use cases (e.g., WatchTower architecture, Slack bot setup, JetBrains integration specifics)
